e.g.

https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/129c6e57052eb243241b5d2d8b7ccb8a8bb3d9ae/numpy/_core/src/multiarray/common.c#L188-L198

here parameter names are missing:

```diff
--- a/numpy/_core/src/multiarray/common.c
+++ b/numpy/_core/src/multiarray/common.c
@@ -188,9 +188,9 @@ _IsWriteable(PyArrayObject *ap)
 /**
  * Convert an array shape to a string such as "(1, 2)".
  *
- * @param Dimensionality of the shape
- * @param npy_intp pointer to shape array
- * @param String to append after the shape `(1, 2)%s`.
+ * @param n Dimensionality of the shape
+ * @param vals npy_intp pointer to shape array
+ * @param ending String to append after the shape `(1, 2)%s`.
  *
  * @return Python unicode string
  */
```

https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/129c6e57052eb243241b5d2d8b7ccb8a8bb3d9ae/numpy/_core/include/numpy/dtype_api.h#L262-L282

here `nargs` seems to have been split into `nin` and `nout`.

### Idea or request for content:

Fix the C docstrings.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27377/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27377/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2389808583","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27377#issuecomment-2389808583","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27377","id":2389808583,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86OcZXH","user":{"login":"eagunn","id":6098961,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwOTg5NjE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6098961?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn","html_url":"https://github.com/eagunn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-02T22:42:03Z","updated_at":"2024-10-02T22:42:03Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I'm ready to create a PR for my changes to address this issue. But I could use some guidance on what "skip" flags to use.
As @mattip clarified above, these C docstrings are not used to build any documentation artifacts; they serve only as internal guidance for developers. So the risk from committing my code, if I've mangled a source code file, would be to the C code build, not to the documentation. And there are no functional changes made by changing comments so unit testing would appear to be unnecessary. 

So I'm thinking I should use the following flags on my PR:
[skip azp] [skip circle] [skip cirrus]
If I understand the skip flags, that would trigger the GitHub actions/builds but neither the unit tests nor  thedocumentation creation.

{"action":"opened","number":27497,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27497","id":2106016121,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAA3dP859h0F5","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27497","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27497.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27497.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27497","number":27497,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"DOC: Correct selected C docstrings to eliminate warnings","user":{"login":"eagunn","id":6098961,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwOTg5NjE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6098961?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn","html_url":"https://github.com/eagunn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Addresses #27377, eliminating all C docstring warning messages.

This is a minimal set of changes, simply eliminating the observed warning messages, with no attempt to improve C docstring content overall, per advice of @mattip.

Reviewers, 
- Some changes were straightforward:
-- The name of the `@param` variable to be documented was missing. I inserted it.
-- The name of the `@param` variable was misspelled. I corrected it.
-- An `@param` line documented a variable that no longer existed or had been split into two. I made the obvious fix.
-- In one case, I removed a docstring from an .h file that simply duplicated the docstring in the corresponding .c file.
- The changes to pay more attention to are those where I had to supply missing Description text. For those, I simply took my best shot and used as few words as I could, figuring the less I wrote, the fewer things would be said incorrectly.

To verify the error messages were being eliminated, I used the same commands as were documented in the original issue but piped the sorted, unique warnings to a file which I diffed against the original list as I worked.
$ CC=clang CXX=clang++ CFLAGS=-Wdocumentation pip install --log PIP.log .
$ grep -- -Wdocumentation PIP.log | sed -E -e 's/^\S+\s+//' | sort -n | uniq > warnings.list

The current `numpy\doc\source\doxyfile` was apparently generated by doxygen version 1.8.18, according to its first line:
`# Doxyfile 1.8.18`
The doxygen version running from my recently-built conda numpy-dev environment is: `1.10.0`

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-10-03T17:15:42Z","updated_at":"2024-10-07T17:24:46Z","closed_at":"2024-10-07T06:59:47Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27497","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27497","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27497.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27497.patch","merged_at":"2024-10-07T06:59:47Z"},"body":"Addresses #27377, eliminating all C docstring warning messages.

This is a minimal set of changes, simply eliminating the observed warning messages, with no attempt to improve C docstring content overall, per advice of @mattip.

Reviewers, 
- Some changes were straightforward:
-- The name of the `@param` variable to be documented was missing. I inserted it.
-- The name of the `@param` variable was misspelled. I corrected it.
-- An `@param` line documented a variable that no longer existed or had been split into two. I made the obvious fix.
-- In one case, I removed a docstring from an .h file that simply duplicated the docstring in the corresponding .c file.
- The changes to pay more attention to are those where I had to supply missing Description text. For those, I simply took my best shot and used as few words as I could, figuring the less I wrote, the fewer things would be said incorrectly.

To verify the error messages were being eliminated, I used the same commands as were documented in the original issue but piped the sorted, unique warnings to a file which I diffed against the original list as I worked.
$ CC=clang CXX=clang++ CFLAGS=-Wdocumentation pip install --log PIP.log .
$ grep -- -Wdocumentation PIP.log | sed -E -e 's/^\S+\s+//' | sort -n | uniq > warnings.list

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-21T14:02:38Z","updated_at":"2024-10-21T14:02:38Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

As of this writing: 
- The CircleCI build uses Doxygen version 1.9.1 [1]
    - Doxygen is installed in the `install system dependencies` step in the `numpy\.circleci\config.yml` file which does not specify either an exact or a minimum version, simply `sudo apt-get install <many other packages> doxygen`
    - I haven't been able to figure out what package repository is used.
    - This build generates one obsolete tag warning.
- Building a new conda numpy-dev environment installs Doxygen 1.10
    - This is specified in the dependencies section of `numpy\environment.yml`, but neither an exact nor a minimum version is specified
    - The package repository is conda-forge [2] so we simply get whatever version they are doling out today.
    - This version generates several obsolete tag warnings, see #27515.
- Our dev documentation [3]:
   - Implies that the developer needs to install Doxygen 'by hand' when, in fact, it is installed as part of the conda numpy-dev environment.
   - Has a note: `Try to install a newer version of Doxygen > 1.8.10 otherwise you may get some warnings during the build.` 
- The current stable version of Doxygen, if one were to download it from their website, is 1.12 [4]

I recommend that, at a minimum:
- The `Dependencies` section of the dev doc be updated to acknowledge/recommend that doxygen is installed as part of the conda environment 
- The note in [3] be changed to acknowledge that obsolete tag warnings will be observed from time to time and may generally be ignored

For the best reproducibility between build environments, we might want to constrain the Doxygen version more tightly, specifying the same version in both the circleci config.yml and the conda environment.yml. I do understand that a) version constraints require downstream maintenance and b) this may be a minor issue within a larger conversation on reproducibility.

Why I think constraining the Doxygen version might be worth the maintenance: 
- Newcomers are often advised to pick out a documentation issue to work on as a first problem. They are likely to notice and be concerned about build warnings.
- Some parts of the documentation toolchain have been reported to be relatively brittle [4]. Constraining versions might keep newcomers and other humans from running into known problems that have been eliminated by how the circleci build is currently configured.

[1] CircleCI Build log:  https://app.circleci.com/pipelines/github/numpy/numpy/29054/workflows/1ec6c92c-a173-4a73-9080-2cf6f8b220d8/jobs/42939?invite=true#step-107-1162_27
[2] Condaforge download page for Doxygen: https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/doxygen
[3] How to build the docs: https://numpy.org/doc/stable/dev/howto_build_docs.html
[4] Doxygen download page: https://www.doxygen.nl/download.html
[5] Documentation tool chain 'fragile': https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27377#issuecomment-2376281668

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-01-05T16:05:53Z","updated_at":"2024-10-21T14:09:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

I had problems repeatedly building PDF docs which various versions of Sphinx, so issueing `make clean`  in-between (EDIT: which is not a priori mandatory, but turns out to be safer).  Turns out that the Doxygen step is triggered by `make html`, not by `make latex`. So in a clean empty state, `make latex` will report a build error (which one understands later to be originating in missing XML files from Doxygen).



### Idea or request for content:

Explanations at https://numpy.org/doc/1.24/dev/howto_build_docs.html#instructions are not so clear about that.  Perhaps the Makefile could contain a `Doxygen` target which would be a dependency of the `latex`  target, or the documentation could explicitly say that one needs to execute at least once `make html`.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27610","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27610/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27610/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27610/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27610","id":2602664884,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86bIYO0","number":27610,"title":"DOC: Controlling (or better documenting) the required/recommended Doxygen version","user":{"login":"eagunn","id":6098961,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwOTg5NjE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6098961?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn","html_url":"https://github.com/eagunn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/eagunn/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359990,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5OTkw","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/04%20-%20Documentation","name":"04 - Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-10-21T14:02:38Z","updated_at":"2024-10-21T17:38:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

As of this writing: 
- The CircleCI build uses Doxygen version 1.9.1 [1]
    - Doxygen is installed in the `install system dependencies` step in the `numpy\.circleci\config.yml` file which does not specify either an exact or a minimum version, simply `sudo apt-get install <many other packages> doxygen`
    - I haven't been able to figure out what package repository is used.
    - This build generates one obsolete tag warning.
- Building a new conda numpy-dev environment installs Doxygen 1.10
    - Doxygen is specified in the dependencies section of `numpy\environment.yml`, but neither an exact nor a minimum version is specified
    - The package repository is conda-forge [2] so we simply get whatever version they are doling out today.
    - This version generates several obsolete tag warnings, see #27515.
- Our dev documentation [3]:
   - Implies that the developer needs to install Doxygen 'by hand' when, in fact, it is installed as part of the conda numpy-dev environment.
   - Has a note: `Try to install a newer version of Doxygen > 1.8.10 otherwise you may get some warnings during the build.` 
- The current stable version of Doxygen, if one were to download it from their website, is 1.12 [4]

I recommend that, at a minimum:
- The `Dependencies` section of the dev doc be updated to acknowledge/recommend that doxygen is installed as part of the conda environment 
- The note in [3] be changed to acknowledge that obsolete tag warnings will be observed from time to time and may generally be ignored

For the best reproducibility between build environments, we might want to constrain the Doxygen version more tightly, specifying the same version in both the circleci config.yml and the conda environment.yml. I do understand that a) version constraints require downstream maintenance and b) this may be a minor issue within a larger conversation on reproducibility.

Why I think constraining the Doxygen version might be worth the maintenance: 
- Newcomers are often advised to pick out a documentation issue to work on as a first problem. They are likely to notice and be concerned about build warnings.
- Some parts of the documentation toolchain have been reported to be relatively brittle [4]. Constraining versions might keep newcomers and other humans from running into known problems that have been eliminated by how the circleci build is currently configured.

[1] CircleCI Build log:  https://app.circleci.com/pipelines/github/numpy/numpy/29054/workflows/1ec6c92c-a173-4a73-9080-2cf6f8b220d8/jobs/42939?invite=true#step-107-1162_27
[2] Condaforge download page for Doxygen: https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/doxygen
[3] How to build the docs: https://numpy.org/doc/stable/dev/howto_build_docs.html
[4] Doxygen download page: https://www.doxygen.nl/download.html
[5] Documentation tool chain 'fragile': https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27377#issuecomment-2376281668

Are you saying that our doc build process:
- Requires and installs doxygen and breathe
- Runs doxygen over the .c/.cpp files to generate the XML output found in numpy\doc\build\doxygen\xml
- Does (or does not, I'm still not clear on this) run Breathe to convert that XML to RST
- In any case, abandons the output from .c/.cpp and does not feed it into the rest of the API documentation that is generated from Python code.

With the effect that:
- Any of the pages of API documentation found at or below file://wsl.localhost/Ubuntu/home/eagunn/openSource/numpy/doc/build/html/reference/c-api/index.html were generated straight from .rst input and were not generated from code and
- All of the Doxygen warning and version issues that I've been chewing on the edges of with my last couple of new issues are simply noise from a currently unused toolset and dead-end in the build process.

If so

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/34a0a7bb-faf0-491d-9514-8fd48733b62b)

It is only if I do not run Doxygen at all that I get the same error message as on the stable/deployed page:
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1a37f5b8-1c94-4fd0-8bf1-1f5febb81e9f)

I'll see if I can figure out what is up with the official build. 

Meantime, if we decided to simply pull the Doxygen step out of the doc build process, then the whole section of that page
https://numpy.org/doc/stable/dev/howto-docs.html#documenting-c-c-code
that shows errors would go away. Now it not only shows errors but documents a process that is not working.

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-10-21T14:02:38Z","updated_at":"2024-10-24T19:50:51Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

As of this writing: 
- The CircleCI build uses Doxygen version 1.9.1 [1]
    - Doxygen is installed in the `install system dependencies` step in the `numpy\.circleci\config.yml` file which does not specify either an exact or a minimum version, simply `sudo apt-get install <many other packages> doxygen`
    - I haven't been able to figure out what package repository is used.
    - This build generates one obsolete tag warning.
- Building a new conda numpy-dev environment installs Doxygen 1.10
    - Doxygen is specified in the dependencies section of `numpy\environment.yml`, but neither an exact nor a minimum version is specified
    - The package repository is conda-forge [2] so we simply get whatever version they are doling out today.
    - This version generates several obsolete tag warnings, see #27515.
- Our dev documentation [3]:
   - Implies that the developer needs to install Doxygen 'by hand' when, in fact, it is installed as part of the conda numpy-dev environment.
   - Has a note: `Try to install a newer version of Doxygen > 1.8.10 otherwise you may get some warnings during the build.` 
- The current stable version of Doxygen, if one were to download it from their website, is 1.12 [4]

I recommend that, at a minimum:
- The `Dependencies` section of the dev doc be updated to acknowledge/recommend that doxygen is installed as part of the conda environment 
- The note in [3] be changed to acknowledge that obsolete tag warnings will be observed from time to time and may generally be ignored

For the best reproducibility between build environments, we might want to constrain the Doxygen version more tightly, specifying the same version in both the circleci config.yml and the conda environment.yml. I do understand that a) version constraints require downstream maintenance and b) this may be a minor issue within a larger conversation on reproducibility.

Why I think constraining the Doxygen version might be worth the maintenance: 
- Newcomers are often advised to pick out a documentation issue to work on as a first problem. They are likely to notice and be concerned about build warnings.
- Some parts of the documentation toolchain have been reported to be relatively brittle [4]. Constraining versions might keep newcomers and other humans from running into known problems that have been eliminated by how the circleci build is currently configured.

[1] CircleCI Build log:  https://app.circleci.com/pipelines/github/numpy/numpy/29054/workflows/1ec6c92c-a173-4a73-9080-2cf6f8b220d8/jobs/42939?invite=true#step-107-1162_27
[2] Condaforge download page for Doxygen: https://anaconda.org/conda-forge/doxygen
[3] How to build the docs: https://numpy.org/doc/stable/dev/howto_build_docs.html
[4] Doxygen download page: https://www.doxygen.nl/download.html
[5] Documentation tool chain 'fragile': https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27377#issuecomment-2376281668

When I look at `numpy\_core\src\multiarray\common.c`, I see ten functions. A couple function have malformed docstrings which generate the warnings cited above. But for most of the functions there are no docstrings at all. However all of them are annotated with with `NPY_NO_EXPORT` which may imply that they are not part of the public API. 

Questions:

- Does `NPY_NO_EXPORT` actually mean the function is private and not public? (A comment at the top of common.c states the typedef is misnamed and actually means the same as `NPY_SAME_KIND_CASTING`.)
- If a function is private, should it still have a docstring?
- If a function is private and should have a docstring, should the docstring be in the .c file and not the .h file? (common.h has at least one of the same malformed docstrings as common.c)
- And, finally, should I simply be trying to make the reported warning messages "go away" or should I be trying to do something more general in the way of a docstring cleanup in this code?

As I was proofing some other changes, I noticed that the Copyright line at the end of the published NEPS is hardcoded to 2017-2018.
![image](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/assets/6098961/8a115ada-a2ac-4b15-8e7c-a919eca21b89)

The main documentation more appropriately has a Copyright line that goes from 2008 to 2024.
![image](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/assets/6098961/29ef7e4c-94e6-4186-bfee-e2ba0bc70618)

This output is controlled by a copyright variable in the applicable conf.py file.

The NEPs' copyright variable in `doc/neps/conf.py1` is
`copyright = '2017-2018, NumPy Developers'`

I believe it should either be changed to be the same as the copyright variable in `doc/source/conf.py` which is:
`copyright = '2008-2024, NumPy Developers'`

OR, to simplify maintenance, it should be made dynamic using syntax something like:
```
import datetime
year = datetime.datetime.now().year
copyright = f'© 2008-{year}, NumPy Developers.'
```
(I haven't tested the above lines; they are adapted from ChatGPT code.)

I'd be happy to try making the dynamic date version of the change to the NEP conf.py since I'm working on those files now. It looks as if the appropriate start year for NEPs would be 2017. That's the current value and   appears to be the publication year of NEP 0.

I had been unaware that, since 1989 (the date cited in your StackOverflow thread above and in [an informative article from the Copyright Alliance](https://copyrightalliance.org/education/copyright-law-explained/copyright-basics/what-is-copyright-notice/)), the whole copyright line is now considered optional. 

However, based on my experience as a content creator and sometime web dev, it is still a common convention to have one. I believe it provides potentially useful information to anyone who, via a link or a search, lands on a page without navigating there from the documentation home page. It's also conventional, in my experience, to use the format, on web pages, that asserts copyright through a range of years even though, technically, copyright applies only to individual instantiations of a work. 

I think it is important to eliminate the need for the conf.py value to be updated every year. I like the idea of using the dynamic date value that Python affords us but I don't feel strongly about that.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2206793741/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

This is a draft only. I will need some feedback on my edits before this change is ready for CI. @HaoZeke, when you have time could you review, using my list below to point out changes that may surprise or displease you.

[skip actions] [skip azp] [skip cirrus]

### Context

This change is not as simple or local as I originally expected. 

- In f2py/buildtools/index.rst, I pulled the distutils text near the top but then had a stab of conscience and added a brief note toward the bottom with a breadcrumb leading back to the last stable version that contained the distutils info. I think it is because you do not assign the results of the reshape to a variable and then explicitly have that variable print out. That is, your examples looks like:
```
    >>> a = np.ma.array([1, 2, 3, 4])
    >>> np.ma.reshape(a, (2, 2))
    masked_array(
      data=[[1, 2],
            [3, 4]],
      mask=False,
      fill_value=999999)
```
and I think they would be easier to understand if they looked like:
```
    >>> a = np.ma.array([1, 2, 3, 4])
    >>> x = np.ma.reshape(a, (2, 2))
    >>> x
    masked_array(
      data=[[1, 2],
            [3, 4]],
      mask=False,
      fill_value=999999)
```

As I was proofing some other changes, I noticed that the Copyright line at the end of the published NEPS is hardcoded to 2017-2018.
![image](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/assets/6098961/8a115ada-a2ac-4b15-8e7c-a919eca21b89)

The main documentation more appropriately has a Copyright line that goes from 2008 to 2024.
![image](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/assets/6098961/29ef7e4c-94e6-4186-bfee-e2ba0bc70618)

This output is controlled by a copyright variable in the applicable conf.py file.

The NEPs' copyright variable in `doc/neps/conf.py1` is
`copyright = '2017-2018, NumPy Developers'`

I believe it should either be changed to be the same as the copyright variable in `doc/source/conf.py` which is:
`copyright = '2008-2024, NumPy Developers'`

OR, to simplify maintenance, it should be made dynamic using syntax something like:
```
import datetime
year = datetime.datetime.now().year
copyright = f'© 2008-{year}, NumPy Developers.'
```
(I haven't tested the above lines; they are adapted from ChatGPT code.)

I'd be happy to try making the dynamic date version of the change to the NEP conf.py since I'm working on those files now. It looks as if the appropriate start year for NEPs would be 2017. That's the current value and   appears to be the publication year of NEP 0.



### Idea or request for content:

However, the code chunks involved here are 3-4 line snippets in the documentation section of the file, intended to demonstrate the use of one function or technique at a time. These should be canonical examples; what any of us who teach coding might put in our lecture notes or type out during a demonstration in front of a class. If they did NOT match other code available out on the net, either character for character or within a few characters, I'd be more concerned.

Meanwhile, I hope that @otieno-juma, @bmwoodruff, and the rest of the POSSE (sp?) team are getting the basic content feedback that they need. Are the variable names clear enough? Should there be comments within the example? Should strings such as 'i4' be assigned to a variable with a meaningful name before being used so it is clearer what role that value is playing in the example. Unfortunately, I can't do any of that for this example because I, frankly, have no idea what the code is doing.

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-07-02T21:58:21Z","updated_at":"2024-07-13T17:30:40Z","closed_at":"2024-07-13T17:30:40Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

As I was proofing some other changes, I noticed that the Copyright line at the end of the published NEPS is hardcoded to 2017-2018.
![image](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/assets/6098961/8a115ada-a2ac-4b15-8e7c-a919eca21b89)

The main documentation more appropriately has a Copyright line that goes from 2008 to 2024.
![image](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/assets/6098961/29ef7e4c-94e6-4186-bfee-e2ba0bc70618)

This output is controlled by a copyright variable in the applicable conf.py file.

The NEPs' copyright variable in `doc/neps/conf.py1` is
`copyright = '2017-2018, NumPy Developers'`

I believe it should either be changed to be the same as the copyright variable in `doc/source/conf.py` which is:
`copyright = '2008-2024, NumPy Developers'`

OR, to simplify maintenance, it should be made dynamic using syntax something like:
```
import datetime
year = datetime.datetime.now().year
copyright = f'© 2008-{year}, NumPy Developers.'
```
(I haven't tested the above lines; they are adapted from ChatGPT code.)

I'd be happy to try making the dynamic date version of the change to the NEP conf.py since I'm working on those files now. It looks as if the appropriate start year for NEPs would be 2017. That's the current value and   appears to be the publication year of NEP 0.

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-07-02T21:58:21Z","updated_at":"2024-07-13T17:31:13Z","closed_at":"2024-07-13T17:30:40Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

As I was proofing some other changes, I noticed that the Copyright line at the end of the published NEPS is hardcoded to 2017-2018.
![image](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/assets/6098961/8a115ada-a2ac-4b15-8e7c-a919eca21b89)

The main documentation more appropriately has a Copyright line that goes from 2008 to 2024.
![image](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/assets/6098961/29ef7e4c-94e6-4186-bfee-e2ba0bc70618)

This output is controlled by a copyright variable in the applicable conf.py file.

The NEPs' copyright variable in `doc/neps/conf.py1` is
`copyright = '2017-2018, NumPy Developers'`

I believe it should either be changed to be the same as the copyright variable in `doc/source/conf.py` which is:
`copyright = '2008-2024, NumPy Developers'`

OR, to simplify maintenance, it should be made dynamic using syntax something like:
```
import datetime
year = datetime.datetime.now().year
copyright = f'© 2008-{year}, NumPy Developers.'
```
(I haven't tested the above lines; they are adapted from ChatGPT code.)

I'd be happy to try making the dynamic date version of the change to the NEP conf.py since I'm working on those files now. It looks as if the appropriate start year for NEPs would be 2017. That's the current value and   appears to be the publication year of NEP 0.

Many users search for these, so webmasters often produce them to drive SEO traffic. 

This is a lightly curated list, in no particular order after the first entry. There are a fair number of web pages out there that are returned based on a search for 'numpy cheatsheet' that aren't cheatsheets at all but either tutorials or simply blogs. I've whittled my results down to pages that return actual cheatsheets, for download or for use online-only. All are 1 page pdfs except as noted.

- datacamp pdf, includes a small number of visuals that look as if they'd be quite helpful: 
https://www.datacamp.com/cheat-sheet/numpy-cheat-sheet-data-analysis-in-python 
This was identified by the OpenAI chatbot and Edge Co-Pilot as the best cheatsheet available.
- geeks for geeks pdf: https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/numpy-cheat-sheet/ 
- personal github md page: https://github.com/jin-zhe/cheatsheets/blob/master/NumPy.md
- quickref.me webpage only: https://quickref.me/numpy.html (no pictures, seems a bit formless to me)
- IntelliPaat python for data science numpy: https://cdn.intellipaat.com/mediaFiles/2018/12/Python-NumPy-Cheat-Sheet-.pdf
- datasciencefree.org: http://datasciencefree.com/numpy.pdf
- dataquest.io (dense!): https://s3.amazonaws.com/dq-blog-files/numpy-cheat-sheet.pdf
- hyperpolyglot.org: side by side reference with MATLAB, R, NumPy, and Julia:
- https://hyperpolyglot.org/numerical-analysis 
- ipgp.github.io, long sheet with pure Python, NumPy, and Matplotlib sections:
- https://ipgp.github.io/scientific_python_cheat_sheet/?utm_content=buffer7d821&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter.com&utm_campaign=buffer#numpy-import-numpy-as-np 
- finxter.com: https://blog.finxter.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/03/CheatSheet-Python-7_-NumPy.pdf
- dev.to, web page only: https://dev.to/jagroop2001/numpy-cheetsheet-168o 
- cheatography: https://cheatography.com/lavanya-m22/cheat-sheets/numpy/ 
(both a pdf and latex source are available, content appears to be generated by some user with the help of a tool)

But as I'm finding and changing the hardlinks, I end up with some questions about the recommended best practice for how to use :ref: overall.

@Mukulikaa above recommended using the syntax:
```:ref:`NEP 50 <NEP50>` ```
which correctly generates a link to the doc\neps\nep-0050-scalar-promotion.html file with the label *NEP 50*. And there are a lot of links like that throughout all the docs.

But another option is to simplify the :ref: to
```:ref:`NEP50` ```
which generates the same hyperlink but pulls its label out of the NEP doc, in this case: *NEP 50 — Promotion rules for Python scalars*

As a newcomer to the docs, I would find it helpful to see the full title for the NEP.  But at the moment, whether to use the built-in document label or a shortened one seems to be left as an option for the author of the document that makes the reference. 

This all adds up to three questions for me:
- Is there currently a best practice policy about what label to use for a link to a NEP  (or to other pages that embed a clear label)? 
- If not, would it make sense to have a policy where the NEP's internal label is used for a first reference within a page and the short version is used for additional references, if any?
- Should I confine my current set of changes only to the hardlinks that were the original problem reported and not make any move to change other NEP links in the same docs to be more consistent?






","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2190040147/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

NumPy itself has switched to Meson. I thought I'd add one or more comments/questions here as breadcrumbs for anyone who decides to tackle this Task.>

When I grep through the .HTML files generated from my doc build, I see numpy.distutils appear on a huge number of pages. But that turns out to be only in the TOC sidebar.

Question: Would a small but significant step in the right direction be to simply remove `numpy.distutils <distutils>` from the `toctree::` section of `numpy\doc\source\reference\module_structure.rst`? {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26478","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26478/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26478/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26478/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26478","id":2304445901,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAA3dP85v4TxN","number":26478,"title":"DOC: Set default as `-j 1` for spin docs and move `-W` to SPHINXOPTS","user":{"login":"bmwoodruff","id":1594818,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1OTQ4MTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1594818?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bmwoodruff","html_url":"https://github.com/bmwoodruff","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bmwoodruff/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bmwoodruff/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bmwoodruff/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bmwoodruff/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bmwoodruff/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bmwoodruff/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bmwoodruff/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bmwoodruff/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bmwoodruff/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359990,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5OTkw","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/04%20-%20Documentation","name":"04 - Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-05-19T05:53:53Z","updated_at":"2024-05-20T14:16:50Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/26478","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26478","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26478.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26478.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This commit tries to solve two problems.
- The pydata_sphinx_theme extension warning can be avoided by setting the default `--jobs` to `1`. This matches `spin test`.
- The `-W` option is currently hardcoded in ALLSPHINXOPTS and impossible to override. This commit moves `-W` to SPHINXOPTS which allows a local machine to remove -W if needed, as described in the documentation. This adds to the discussion in PR #26125.

[skip actions] [skip azp] [skip cirrus]

### Context
While trying to build a workflow that incorporates `spin docs`, I encountered two issues. First, I noticed that the the following suggestion in the spin documentation was invalid. 
https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/fba4fc16a26e77ec4ce2b1900577f088b1d5a7a6/.spin/cmds.py#L142-L145
The fact that `-W` is hard coded in `ALLSPHINXOPTS` makes it impossible to remove without changing the makefile. 

The second issue is the [known](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26125#issuecomment-2112509451) error message 
```
WARNING: the pydata_sphinx_theme extension is not safe for parallel writing
WARNING: doing serial write
```
This was [fixed](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26125/commits/79e5c870743f2dc532b4f02cebbfb36efb4206b0) for those using `make` by removing `-j2` from `SPHINXOPTS`. The circle build uses `make` but new users are [directed](https://numpy.org/devdocs/dev/howto_build_docs.html) to use `spin docs`. When someone runs `SPHINXOPTS="-n" make -e html` everything works fine. When someone runs `spin docs`, the generated doc build command is 
```
LANG=C sphinx-build -b html -WT --keep-going -d build/doctrees  -j auto source build/html
```
The problem is `-j auto`.  Running `SPHINXOPTS="-j 1" spin docs` generates 
```
LANG=C sphinx-build -b html -WT --keep-going -d build/doctrees  -j 1 -j auto source build/html
```
The problem remains. Using `spin docs -j 1` instead of `SPHINXOPTS="-j 1" spin docs` works. Discovering this fix was difficult for a newcomer, as the rest of the examples in the codebase model changing SPHINXOPTS.

I set up 7 different codespace environments, and sometimes the docs would build without errors, while other times they would fail. My best guess now is that this may have to do with how many cores `-j auto` was able to access on each different codespace. 

### Further Discussion
I'd love to change something to help newcomers so they don't have to question why their doc build gives an error message (that they can ignore). Here's some options.
- Swapping the default to `1`, and later updating it back to auto when an upstream fix is found, would simplify things for newcomers. This PR follows this option. I purposefully matched the `default` and `help` from `spin test`.  
- Alternately, we could add a note in the docs wherever `spin docs` appears that adds a comment to use `spin docs -j 1` if they encounter a warning. IMHO, this makes it harder for newcomers to contribute. If anything, this PR will provide a record for people to find a fix. 
- We could test if the pydata_sphinx_theme extension is safe for parallel writing (they fixed things upstream), and then set the default based on that. This muddies up `cmds.py`, but does mean the change to `auto` will auto happen.

As for the `-W` option change, I understand why we want the doc build to throw an error on numpy/numpy.  Moving `-W` to `SPHINXOPTS` allows both upstream and local repos to set the option as needed.  
- I left `doc/neps/makefile` alone. The `-W` command is still in ALLSPHINXOPTS. I can update that file as well, if wanted.

I just spent several hours across three days trying to get `spin docs` to run to completion without an error. I determined fairly early that the pydata_sphinx_theme extension warning was "just a warning" and that all of the doc files were being correctly generated. But I was trying to play by the rules and either a) eliminate the warning or b) "correctly" alter the treat-warnings-as-errors option.

I never hit upon `spin docs -j 1` (to eliminate the warnings). I did try `SPHINXOPTS="" spin docs` (to eliminate the terminal error message) which, as @bmwoodruff documents, did not work as indicated. In the end, I was able to pull the `make` command out of the spin build log and run it from the commandline, outside of `spin`, where it worked fine. I finally decided, reluctantly, to simply ignore the warnings and the terminal error and start making my changes to docs.

Please accept the default `-j 1` to fix the build process so it works consistently across all environments. (Mine is WSL Ubuntu where it generated warnings always.  Having it generate warnings randomly in codespaces is worse; that's the dependable environment recommended for newbies so they don't have to worry about setup.) 

I also agree completely with:
>_As for the -W option change, I understand why we want the doc build to throw an error on numpy/numpy. Moving -W to SPHINXOPTS allows both upstream and local repos to set the option as needed._

I'm completely new to this project but I've been on teams where warnings were ignored and we always paid the price eventually. Warnings-as-errors is the right default, but individuals should be able to successfully override the -W setting as needed during their local development.

is an installment in addressing ticket #25597, tackling only the f2py documentation.

This is a draft only. I will need some feedback on my edits before this change is ready for CI. @HaoZeke, when you have time could you review, using my list below to point out changes that may surprise or displease you.

[skip ci] [skip actions] [skip azp] [skip cirrus]

### Context

This change is not as simple or local as I originally expected. 

- In f2py/buildtools/index.rst, I pulled the distutils text near the top but then had a stab of conscience and added a brief note toward the bottom with a breadcrumb leading back to the last stable version that contained the distutils info. It seemed potentially disorienting to write as if distutils had never existed. But I'll be happy to pull the note if it is deemed unnecessary.
- I changed the note at the top of f2py/buildtools/meson.rst to make it more coherent (to me). 
- I had to change two files outside of the f2py/buildtools folder in order to delete references to the now-deleted content.
- (Mea culpa in advance. Simply say if this is a change too far.) I'm not set up to build and test. I was just cruising through the issues deciding if this was a project where I might be able to make contributions in the future. But, since I think I've figured out what is wrong with the PyDoc comments, I thought I would leave a note here to possibly make the eventual fix faster for the next in line. 

I looks as if the documentation for these classes is stuck between two approaches to documenting exponents of e. See [https://tex.stackexchange.com/questions/254785/e-vs-exp-in-display-mode](https://tex.stackexchange.com/questions/254785/e-vs-exp-in-display-mode) for a helpful discussion of the two approaches.

Specifically, I believe the raw equation in the Pydoc:
`x_{k} = (b-a) + \\frac{\\exp{\\alpha k / N} - 1}{\\exp{\\alpha} - 1} + a,`
should be more like
`x_{k} = (b-a) + \\frac{\\exp(\\alpha k / N) - 1}{\\exp(\\alpha} - 1) + a,`

That is, the Tex operator \exp is to be followed by the expression in parentheses, not curly brackets.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pybamm-team/PyBaMM/issues/comments/1373754876/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
